mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Belize on?
mapQuestions.answer: North America (Central America). Belize sits on the Caribbean coast, with Mexico to the north and Guatemala to the south and west 🌎

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Belize?
mapQuestions.answer: Belmopan, in the centre. A small (15,000 people) planned capital city. The biggest city is Belize City on the coast, where most people live.

mapQuestions.question: What languages does Belize speak?
mapQuestions.answer: ENGLISH is the official language (Belize was British Honduras until 1981, the only English-speaking country in Central America). Belizean Creole, Spanish, Garifuna and Mayan languages are also widely spoken.

mapQuestions.question: What is the Belize Barrier Reef?
mapQuestions.answer: The world's SECOND-largest coral reef (after Australia's Great Barrier Reef), 300 km long, just off the coast. UNESCO World Heritage. Home to whale sharks, turtles, sharks and 500+ fish species.

mapQuestions.question: What is the Great Blue Hole?
mapQuestions.answer: A perfectly round, deep-blue underwater sinkhole, 300 m wide and 124 m deep, in the middle of the Belize reef. Made famous by Jacques Cousteau as one of the world's top dive sites.

foods.name: Rice and beans
foods.description: A coconut-milk rice cooked together with red kidney beans, served with stewed chicken, beef or fish. The Belizean Sunday lunch and unofficial national dish.

foods.name: Recado rojo / chimole
foods.description: A deep-red Mayan-spice paste of annatto, garlic, cumin and oregano, rubbed over roasted pork or chicken, especially in the north of Belize.

foods.name: Hudut
foods.description: A traditional Garifuna dish: a fish coconut stew served with pounded green-plantain dough. A coastal village classic.

foods.name: Salbutes
foods.description: Crispy puffed corn tortillas topped with shredded chicken or turkey, cabbage, tomato, avocado and a slice of pickled red onion. Mexican-Maya street snacks.

foods.name: Conch fritters
foods.description: Battered, deep-fried conch (a Caribbean sea snail), served with a tangy hot sauce. Eaten at every beach bar.

foods.name: Fry jacks
foods.description: Pillowy puffed deep-fried bread squares, served at breakfast with beans, eggs and stewed beef, or eaten sweet with honey or jam. The classic Belizean breakfast.

scavengerItems.label: A turquoise lagoon at Caye Caulker or Ambergris Caye
A green or hawksbill sea turtle on a coral reef
A nurse shark at Shark Ray Alley in Caye Caulker
A jaguar print in the Cockscomb Basin (the only dedicated jaguar reserve in the world)
Mayan ruins rising above the rainforest (Caracol, Xunantunich, Lamanai)
A keel-billed toucan, Belize's national bird (rainbow beak)
A cave-tubing trip through the Caves Branch system on an inner tube
The dark-blue circle of the Great Blue Hole from above (flightseeing tour)
A bright-blue morpho butterfly fluttering in the jungle
Garifuna drumming and "punta rock" music on the southern coast
A Morelet's crocodile sliding into a river (smaller and more shy than the saltwater kind)

geoMatches.description: The biggest city, on the Caribbean coast. Loud, hot, busy, with old British colonial buildings, a fish market and ferries running to the cayes.
A small reef island with no cars, white sand, hammocks, and the unofficial motto "Go Slow". The Belize Reef is just minutes offshore.
Belize's biggest island, with the town of San Pedro. The famous Hol Chan Marine Reserve and Shark Ray Alley are right offshore.
The biggest Mayan archaeological site in Belize, deep in the rainforest. Sky Palace, the tallest building in Belize, sits on top of a Mayan pyramid.
The "Jaguar Preserve": the only national park anywhere in the world set up specifically to protect jaguars. Around 80 of them roam the rainforest.
A long, narrow palm-fringed peninsula in the south with quiet beaches, a Garifuna community in Seine Bight, and access to the reef.

senses.smell: Coconut oil in rice and beans, salt and seaweed on the cayes, jasmine in the evening, woodsmoke from a beachfront grill, the rich earthy smell of the Belizean jungle after rain…
senses.hear: Belizean Creole flowing fast and rhythmic, "Weh di go aan?" and "Aarait!", reggae and Garifuna punta-rock music from beach bars, howler monkeys roaring at dawn in Cockscomb, parrots squawking in the canopy…
senses.taste: Sweet coconut-milk rice and beans, smoky stewed chicken, lime-bright ceviche, salty fry-jack puffs, fresh tropical fruit (mango, sapote, pineapple) cut on the beach…
senses.feel: Warm Caribbean water (28°C all year), white sand between your toes, the humidity of the rainforest, the cool inside of a Mayan stone temple, the sting of sun on a snorkel session…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How big is the Belize Reef?
stories.body: The Belize Barrier Reef is the SECOND-largest barrier reef on Earth (after Australia's Great Barrier Reef), running 300 km along Belize's entire coast. UNESCO put it on the World Heritage list in 1996. It is home to over 500 species of fish, 100+ kinds of hard coral, and most of the Caribbean's sea turtles, manatees and reef sharks. Charles Darwin himself studied this reef and called it the most remarkable in the Caribbean. Snorkelling on Belize's reef is shockingly easy because much of it is in shallow turquoise water just metres from white-sand cayes. Some patches have been bleached by warmer seas, but conservation work is underway.

stories.question: What is the Great Blue Hole?
stories.body: In the middle of Belize's reef sits a perfectly round, dark-blue sinkhole, 300 m wide and 124 m deep. The Great Blue Hole was originally a limestone cave, formed during ancient ice ages when sea levels were much lower. As the world warmed and seas rose, the cave roof collapsed, and the sea flooded in, leaving the dark perfect circle you see today. The French underwater explorer Jacques Cousteau called it one of the top 10 dive sites in the world in 1971, and ranked it among his all-time favourites. From the air, it looks like a giant blue eye staring up at you out of the turquoise reef.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A whole underground cave system became one of the world's most famous diving sites because the roof collapsed. What other "lucky accidents" have shaped places we love?

stories.question: How do you protect a jaguar?
stories.body: In 1986, the world's first reserve set up specifically for jaguars was created in southern Belize: the Cockscomb Basin Wildlife Sanctuary, 400 square km of dense rainforest. Around 80 jaguars live in it today, the densest population of jaguars anywhere in Central America. Jaguars are notoriously hard to spot (they are nocturnal, solitary, and silent), but you can often find their paw prints on muddy trails. The Belizean naturalist Dr Alan Rabinowitz, who fought to create the reserve, said that one footprint of a jaguar in the mud told him "more than seeing the cat itself". Belize has been a quiet world leader in jaguar conservation ever since.

stories.question: Who are the Garifuna?
stories.body: The Garifuna are a people descended from West African enslaved peoples who escaped a Spanish slave ship in 1635 and mixed with the indigenous Caribs on the island of St Vincent. The British eventually exiled them en masse to Central America in 1797. They settled along the Caribbean coast of what is now Honduras, Guatemala, Nicaragua and Belize. They speak their own Garifuna language (a mix of African, indigenous and European words), have their own music ("punta rock" and traditional drumming), and their own food (especially "hudut" and "ereba" cassava bread). UNESCO put Garifuna culture on its "intangible cultural heritage" list in 2001. The biggest Garifuna communities in Belize are at Dangriga, Hopkins, Seine Bight and Punta Gorda.

stories.question: Why does Belize speak English in Central America?
stories.body: Belize was a British colony called British Honduras from 1862 until 1981. It originally became British because of pirates and loggers cutting precious tropical hardwoods (logwood and mahogany) on the coast in the 1600s and 1700s. The British government formalised the colony in the 1860s. Belize finally became fully independent from Britain in 1981, keeping English as its official language. This makes Belize unusual: every other country in Central America was a Spanish colony and speaks Spanish today. Belizean Creole is a separate English-based creole language that most Belizeans grow up speaking at home.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Belize so far?
Have you tried any Creole or Garifuna phrases yet?
Reef, jungle, ruins, beach, which has felt the most "Belize"?
What did you see on a snorkel that you had never seen before?
How does the "Go Slow" motto of Caye Caulker feel?
Belize has reef, rainforest, Mayan ruins, English, Creole, Spanish, Garifuna, Maya, all in a country smaller than Wales. How does it manage all that diversity?

wordSearch.hint: Belizean foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Green sea turtle
animals.description: Common on the Belize Barrier Reef and in seagrass beds. The Hol Chan Marine Reserve protects them.

animals.name: Nurse shark
animals.description: Slow, harmless reef sharks. Often resting under coral ledges. Shark Ray Alley in Caye Caulker is famous for them.

animals.name: West Indian manatee
animals.description: Slow, gentle sea mammals in the lagoons around Placencia. Look for them grazing on seagrass.

animals.name: Keel-billed toucan
animals.description: The Belize national bird. Massive rainbow beak almost as long as its body.

animals.name: Jaguar
animals.description: Belize has Central America's biggest jaguar population. Cockscomb Basin is the only nature reserve in the world set up specifically to protect them.

animals.name: Morelet's crocodile
animals.description: A smaller, shy freshwater crocodile, in Belize's rivers. Different from the saltwater kind, much less dangerous.

animals.name: Fer-de-lance
animals.description: A venomous pit-viper of the rainforest. Always watch your step on jungle trails.
